text: The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position on the operating table, preparing for the laparoscopic repair of the diaphragmatic hernia.
An 11mm port is placed in the left upper quadrant for the laparoscopic camera, while three additional working ports are positioned along the left costal margin for the surgeon and assistant.
The laparoscopic camera captures the left diaphragmatic defect, which visibly contains the majority of the patient's omentum.
The surgeon identifies and divides the peritoneal lining along the left diaphragmatic defect, preparing the area for further repair.
The surgeon uses either sharp dissection or an energy device to manipulate tissue around the left diaphragmatic defect, which contains incarcerated omentum.
The surgeon performs dissection along the lateral rim of the left diaphragmatic defect, carefully avoiding injury to the hernia contents visible within the defect.
After completing the mobilization and division of the peritoneal attachments, the surgeon gently reduces the hernia contents, which are visible within the left diaphragmatic defect.
The surgeon applies steady downward traction using a hand over hand technique to reduce the hernia contents from the left diaphragmatic defect, which contains the patient's omentum.
The surgeon enlarges the hernia defect by carefully dividing the diaphragm using cautery, creating a wider opening for further surgical access.
The surgeon gently reduces the hernia contents back into the abdominal cavity, following sufficient mobilization and division of peritoneal attachments.
The surgeon observes the surgical field, noting the relatively few interthoracic adhesions present in the patient.
The surgeon explores the left chest cavity using a laparoscopic camera, providing a clear view of the internal structures and any potential adhesions or hernia contents.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigation device to remove bloody loculated effusions from the incarcerated hernia contents, clearing the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suction device to remove the remaining pleural effusion from the left chest cavity, clearing the fluid from the surgical field.
The video shows the left lower lobe of the lung completely collapsed, consistent with previous CT imaging findings.
The surgeon uses a double lumen tube to preferentially ventilate the left lung, resulting in the near complete re-expansion of the collapsed left lower lobe.
The surgeon uses cautery to detach the final attachments of the omentum from the posterior diaphragm, completing the separation of these structures.
The surgeon surveys the hernia defect, visually estimating its size to be approximately three to four centimeters.
The surgeon closes the defect primarily, indicating that the tissue edges are approximated and sutured together without tension.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic suturing device to close the defect with nonabsorbable suture in a running fashion, visible through the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon ties the two tails of the running suture in the middle of the closed defect, securing the repair.
The surgeon takes additional interrupted suture bites to ensure the adequate closure of the defect, which measures approximately three to four centimeters.
After the defect is satisfactorily closed, the surgeon desufflates the abdomen and places a 28 French chest tube into the surgical site.
